data.title: Benchmark JGB yield hits 29-year high on oil surge and inflation fears
data.description:
By Satoshi Sugiyama

Japanese government bond (JGB) yields rose on Thursday, with the benchmark 10-year yield hitting a 29-year high, as a stalemate in U.S.-Iran peace talks drove oil prices higher and fuelled inflation concerns.

The benchmark 10-year JGB yield (JP10YTN=JBTC) rose 5.5 basis points (bps) to 2.515%, the highest since June 1997. The five-year yield (JP5YTN=JBTC) rose 4.5 bps to 1.9%. Yields move inversely to bond prices.

"Higher oil prices and the risk of further deterioration in the situation around the Strait of Hormuz are weighing on the market, leading to stronger selling pressure in the long end of the bond market," said Ryutaro Kimura, fixed-income strategist at BNP Paribas Asset Management.

"It raises the risk of further price increases, and, judging from global market scenarios as well, the current move is likely to heighten inflation concerns."

The 30-year yield (JP30YTN=JBTC) added 5 bps to 3.69%. The yield on the 40-year JGB (JP40YTN=JBTC), Japan's longest tenor, rose 6 bps to 3.925%. Ahead of the finance ministry's auction of two-year JGBs later in the day, the yield on the notes (JP2YTN=JBTC) rose 2 basis points to 1.39%.

Oil prices surged over 6% on Wednesday to settle at their highest in weeks, with deadlocked U.S.-Iran negotiations making investors more concerned about a prolonged disruption to Middle Eastern energy supplies.

Although the U.S. Federal Reserve kept interest rates steady, three policymakers dissented from the central bank's statement, reflecting a deepening split over whether it should continue signalling a bias toward lower borrowing costs. U.S. Treasury yields rose to a one-month high after the release of the policy statement.

data.title: Oil Rises Amid Concerns Over Prolonged U.S.-Iran Conflict
data.description:
By Ronnie Harui

Oil rose in Asia on Thursday amid concerns over a prolonged Middle East conflict as the U.S. seeks to extend the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

President Trump seemed to suggest the possibility that U.S. military action could resume. "Iran can't get their act together. They don't know how to sign a nonnuclear deal. They better get smart soon!," Trump posted Wednesday on Truth Social, accompanied by an image of himself with the slogan "No More Mr. Nice Guy."

Trump told aides on Monday to prepare for an extended blockade of Iran, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing U.S. officials. In recent meetings, Trump decided to continue clamping down on Iran's economy and oil exports by preventing shipping to and from its ports.

Front-month Brent crude oil futures rose 0.4% to $107.27 a barrel and front-month West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures rose 1.5% to $119.81 a barrel, ICE data showed.

"The rally in crude oil prices shows no signs of slowing, as the prospect of an indefinite closure of the Strait of Hormuz weighs on sentiment," ANZ Research analysts said in a note. "The gains suggest investors are bracing for a protracted conflict that could see oil supplies from the Persian Gulf remain curtailed," the analysts added.

Meanwhile, government bonds across Asia fell on dimming prospects of further interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. Although the U.S. central bank held its benchmark federal-funds rate steady in a range of 3.5% to 3.75% on Wednesday, its statement drew dissents from four out of 12 members, the most at a policy meeting since 1992.

Three Fed presidents--Beth Hammack of Cleveland, Neel Kashkari of Minneapolis, and Lorie Logan of Dallas--supported the rate decision, but objected to retaining the "easing bias" that for the last two years has suggested a rate cut is more likely than a rate increase.

"Looking ahead, we expect the next policy move to be a [Fed rate] hike, starting in December, because of higher inflation," said Samara Hammoud, international economist and currency strategist at Commonwealth Bank of Australia, in a note.

The yield on Japan's benchmark 10-year government bond rose 5.5 basis points to 2.515%, the highest intraday level since June 1997, according to data provider Quick. The yield on Australia's 10-year sovereign securities climbed 7 basis points to 5.0670% and the yield on New Zealand's 10-year government bond rose 6 basis points to 4.7610%. Bond yields move inversely to prices.

Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com

data.title: Chinese Airlines Swing to Profit; China Southern Places Airbus Order Worth $21.4 billion
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Two of China's largest airlines swung to profit in the first quarter, with China Southern separately announcing aircraft purchases worth $21.4 billion from Airbus.

China Southern, one of China's three major state-owned carriers, reported a net profit of 1.48 billion yuan, equivalent to $216.4 million, it said late Wednesday. That compares with a net loss of 747 million yuan in the year-earlier period.

The airline attributed the performance to a "sustained improvement in production and operations."

Operating revenue rose 10% to 47.78 billion yuan.

Chinese flag carrier Air China also swung to a profit for the first quarter. It posted a net profit of 1.71 billion yuan, compared with a net loss of 2.04 billion yuan a year earlier.

It attributed the improved profitability to continuous route optimization and an increase in operating income while taking cost control measures.

The results come despite the macroeconomic uncertainties and disruptions to air travel arising from the ongoing Middle East conflict.

China Southern said it and its subsidiary, Xiamen Airlines, plan to buy 137 Airbus A320neo narrow-body aircraft. The deal, which includes negotiated price concessions from Airbus, is intended to "enhance the group's market competitiveness by increasing its flight capacity," it said.

China Southern will purchase 102 jets, and Xiamen Airlines will buy 35 jets. The aircraft deliveries are scheduled in stages from 2028 to 2032.

The state-owned carrier plans to raise up to 15 billion yuan through the issuance of up to 5.44 billion new A-shares to help finance the purchase of the aircraft and supplement general working capital.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: China's factory activity remains in expansion in April, PMI shows
data.description:
China's factory activity expanded for a second straight month in April, an official survey showed on Thursday, suggesting that growth momentum held despite external shocks stemming from the Middle East war.

The official manufacturing purchasing managers' index (PMI) dipped to 50.3 from 50.4 in March, above the 50-mark separating growth from contraction, according to a survey by the National Bureau of Statistics. It beat a median forecast of 50.1 in a Reuters poll.
